I usually don't buy stuff advertised on TV because when you receive the package in the mail, it usually does not appear as well made as it does on TV and doesn't work as well either.

They're for hanging pictures, and they're great because you don't need to put a giant hole in your wall and you don't even need to find a stud to anchor the picture so it doesn't fall off the wall.

Dustin in Casper, Wyoming writes: One night many years ago, when my wife was out of town, I was on the couch flipping through the channels, trying to get some sleep and came across Marshall Sylver, ""Passion, Profit and Power,"" a hypnotist claiming to be able to do anything - from help you stop smoking to miraculous weight loss, to hypnotizing you right through the TV.
When I awoke the next morning and came to my senses, I found I had ordered not just the self-help tapes but also a workout video and a vegetable chopper.
Luckily, all three companies allowed me to return their products once the post-hypnotic suggestion wore off.
Let's see if we can get this last email from Eve.
When my dad passed away, we realized just how much he loved his infomercials.
He had a wide assortment of gems from the Flowbie to the Magic Bullet to the infamous set it and forget it Ron Popeil Rotisserie Oven.

He passed away a year ago, but every time an infomercial, particularly a cooking product, is on, we find ourselves watching and laughing about how much dad would have needed whatever they are selling.
And Remy Stern, what's the one product that you've ordered you could not be without?
I don't know if there is one product that I could not be without.
I think - you know, I sort of love them all in their own sort of special ways.
They're fun products, and it's sort of, I think, a fun little corner of American pop culture just over the last 20 or so years.
And you know, we enjoy watching some of these shows as much as buying the products.
It's entertainment.
But, Wait…There's More!"" That's the name of Remy Stern's book.
It's about the pitchman industry.
Thanks very much for being with us today.
Thank you for having me.
Remy Stern joined us from his office in New York City.
He also edits and publishes the Web site CityLife.com.
From the studios of NPR West, this is DAY TO DAY.
I'm Alex Chadwick.
I'm Madeleine Brand.
Coming up, President Bush responds to Republican critics of his Iraq policy.
First, there is a new study of the number of deaths among Iraqis since the start of the war.
And the figure is very high: about 650,000.
This is from researchers at the Bloomberg School of Public Health at Johns Hopkins University in Baltimore.
It's reported in the British journal, The Lancet.
The researchers interviewed members of nearly 2,000 Iraqi households to reach their number.
The timing of the release of this study is already being attacked as politically motivated, coming as it does just four weeks before the election.
We're joined by NPR science correspondent Richard Harris.
Richard, welcome back to the program.
And this number is more than 10 times the size of previous estimates that we've seen.
How do you get to that number?
Well, the previous estimates were based primarily on news reports.
And that is clearly an under-representation of how many people actually died in Iraq.
Those reports also are civilian deaths - the smaller number -whereas this is total deaths throughout Iraq.
And this is what they did.

They went through, they picked 2,000 randomly selected households in Iraq and they knocked on doors - this is very dangerous work - and they asked people who's been born and who's died in this household in the last few years.
